romania.title: FOTO Proprietarul Muzeului Recordurilor Românești vinde un proiect abandonat de birouri, început acum 20 de ani în Dorobanți
romania.summary: Arhitectul Ion Chirescu, cunoscut și ca proprietar al Muzeului Recordurilor Românești, a scos la vânzare, la un preț de circa 11 milioane de euro, un proiect abandonat de birouri a cărui construcție a fost începută în urmă cu aproximativ 20 de ani și care are finalizate 3 subsoluri pentru parcări în zona bucureșteană Dorobanți. Ion …

romania.title: Mii de români cer băncilor rate mai mici. Dacă acolo au mai găsit înțelegere, IFN-urile nici nu vor să audă - zero cereri aprobate
romania.summary: Mii de români încearcă să-și reducă ratele la bănci. Sunt datele statistice ale centrului care gestionează litigiile dintre bănci și clienți. Dacă în cazul băncilor sute de oameni și-au renegociat contractele de credit, în ceea ce privește IFN-urile, românii s-au lovit de refuzuri categorice.

romania.title: În 17 iulie se deschide unul dintre cele mai cunoscute și urmărite festivaluri de muzică clasică din lume: BBC Proms, cu o istorie de peste 130 ani

Este vorba despre primul magazin Primark din Sibiu, situat în Shopping City Sibiu. Compania vorbește despre o investiție de peste 8 milioane de euro pentru deschiderea acestuia. …

Proiectul educativ a dus în comunitățile din județele Constanța și Brașov ateliere practice… Articolul 2.600 de elevi din mediul rural au descoperit știința prin ateliere practice – EFdeN încheie primele două tururi ale Caravanei Sustenabilității apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Salon des Refusés lansează o ediție specială la Timișoara: Cineclub, proiecții premiate internațional și dialoguri curatoriale la Cinema Studio.
romania.summary: Salon des Refusés, programul curatorial de recuperare și recontextualizare a filmelor de autor contemporane rămase în afara distribuției comerciale, își extinde oficial activitatea la Timișoara. După succesul edițiilor din București… Articolul Salon des Refusés lansează o ediție specială la Timișoara: Cineclub, proiecții premiate internațional și dialoguri curatoriale la Cinema Studio. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Primele explicații ale lui Mihai Jurca după ce a renunțat la televiziune după 21 de ani: „Nu iau niciodată decizii pripite, decizii la nervi, decizii la „cald””
romania.summary: Cunoscutul prezentator al rubricii meteo de la Antena 1 a explicat pentru PaginadeMedia ce se află în spatele schimbării sale de carieră. Mihai Jurca a prezentat ultima sa rubrică meteo în televiziune, urmând ca în perioada următoare să se alăture agenției de turism lansate de Răzvan Pascu, în rolul de tour leader. „Am avut mereu …

romania.title: 😂 Justiţia a decis: biserica ridicată ilegal la Techirghiol trebuie mutată ilegal în altă zonă naturală protejată
romania.summary: Tribunalul Mangalia dă în sfârşit un verdict în cazul bisericii ridicate ilegal pe malul lacului Techirghiol, într-o arie naturală protejată unde construcţiile sunt strict interzise. Potrivit judecătorilor, constructorul lăcaşului de cult ilegal are la dispoziţie 60 de zile să îl mute ilegal în altă rezervaţie naturală, preferabil în alt judeţ, ca să fie problema ălora […] The post Justiţia a decis: biserica ridicată ilegal la Techirghiol trebuie mutată ilegal în altă zonă naturală protejată appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Pe fondul unei cereri din ce în ce mai reduse de maşini second-hand pe pieţe precum România, Albania sau Burkina Faso, conducerea Volkswagen e nevoită să ia măsuri dure! Directorul general al companiei, Pimmel Schwanzhoff, a anunţat recent că va disponibiliza aproape 50.000 de bătrânici care scoteau maşinile din garaj duminica pentru a merge doar […] The post Piaţa maşinilor second, în scădere. VW concediază 50.000 de bătrânici care conduceau doar duminica appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Razie în Vama Veche. A fost prins un bătrân cu 300 de pastile de Antinevralgic la el
romania.summary: Razie DIICOT în iadul boșorogilor din Vama Veche. Polițiștii însoțiți de procurori au capturat mai mulți traficanți de stupefiante, inclusiv un bărbat de 51 de ani, care avea la el 300 de pastile de antinevralgic. Bărbatul a declarat că pastilele sunt pentru consum propriu, pentru că stă într-o cameră cu pereți de rigips și în […] The post Razie în Vama Veche. A fost prins un bătrân cu 300 de pastile de Antinevralgic la el appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
romania.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

medical.title: Six medical societies call for immediate adoption of enhanced radiation protection in fluoroscopy labs
medical.summary: Health care professionals performing minimally invasive procedures in fluoroscopy laboratories, often called "cath labs," should no longer have to accept preventable radiation exposure and orthopedic injuries as part of their jobs, according to an expert consensus statement released today that is endorsed by six leading medical societies.

medical.title: Preterm birth impacts early educational achievements, study finds
medical.summary: More than half (57%) of children born before 32 weeks were not ready for school at age 5, including in areas such as communication and language and physical and emotional development. Those born earlier, at 23–24 weeks, were up to three times more likely to miss expected developmental milestones than those born at 31 weeks.

medical.title: Metabolites produced by gut bacteria may protect against fungal infection
medical.summary: A metabolic byproduct formed when gut bacteria break down dietary fiber appears to protect against dangerous fungal infections common in immunocompromised patients, a study led by UT Southwestern Medical Center researchers shows. The findings, published in Cell Host & Microbe, could lead to new therapies to shield this vulnerable population from Candida albicans, a leading human fungal pathogen.

medical.title: Jude Bellingham's leg veins are standing out as much as his scoring skills: Here's why
medical.summary: The ripple of the net after a Jude Bellingham strike is no longer the only ripple attracting attention. During England's World Cup campaign, the midfielder's prominent thigh veins have become an internet talking point.

medical.title: Early tech training builds clinical confidence
medical.summary: New research from The University of Texas at Arlington suggests that introducing electronic health record training earlier in nurse practitioner programs could help address a significant challenge facing the field—burnout among experienced clinicians who supervise and train future nurse practitioners.

medical.title: First diagnostic X-rays in space mark new era for astronaut health
medical.summary: A team of crew members aboard a commercial spaceflight acquired the first diagnostic X-rays during an orbital flight. Results of the mission were published today in Radiology.

medical.title: The GLP‑1 disclosure dilemma: Should you tell a date you're using Ozempic for weight loss?
medical.summary: As a Gen Xer who lived through the "heroin chic" years, when skeletal models were celebrated for jutting bones and sallow skin, the recent popularity of the #Y2KSkinny trend fills me with dread.

medical.title: There's one reason why so many cold and flu tablets don't actually work
medical.summary: You've got the sniffles, and your nose is running like a faucet. The tissues feel like sandpaper. Many Australians reach for cold and flu tablets, expecting them to "clear the sinuses" and get them through a cold. They often promise fast relief.

medical.title: Popular weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy may slow biological aging
medical.summary: Researchers found that semaglutide, the active ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy, slowed biological aging markers in adults with HIV, marking the first clinical evidence that the drug may influence human aging. Although the findings are encouraging, scientists say larger studies are needed before concluding that the medication can help people age more slowly.

medical.title: What positive change have you seen unfold over many years?
medical.summary: Share the stories of change you’ve witnessed that took patience, persistence and time The post What positive change have you seen unfold over many years? appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: How supermassive black holes feed themselves
science.summary: Astronomers are closer to solving the mystery of how supermassive black holes feed themselves thanks to new images from the James Webb Space Telescope, or JWST. The images provide the clearest view ever seen of gaseous filaments connecting a galaxy's hot atmosphere to the rotating disk of gas that feeds its central supermassive black hole.

science.title: Bio-metal: Exploring the metallic mystery of an ancient maw
science.summary: When playing the classic game "20 Questions," one may begin with the common opener: "Animal, vegetable, or mineral?"

science.title: The customer isn't always right: Study reveals overlooked source of workplace discrimination
science.summary: New research from Adelaide University has found customers can be an overlooked source of workplace discrimination, leaving gay and bisexual men feeling they must choose between being authentic or protecting themselves from prejudice.

science.title: Haven or trap? Study finds sinkholes protect endangered tree at evolutionary cost
science.summary: Are giant sinkholes in China's karst mountains havens or traps for the rare plants that inhabit them? A new study finds they are both—offering refuge from heat and drought while gradually eroding the evolutionary potential of an endangered tree.

science.title: Physicists confirm 20-year-old theory that could boost quantum technology
science.summary: Future quantum computing will require correlations between distant modules—a feature known as distributed entanglement. Traditionally, such entanglement has relied on active control and repeated measurements. Now, physicists at the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA) have realized a fully autonomous method for distributed entanglement using a "quantum bath" of correlated light particles. Published in Physical Review X, their work experimentally confirms a 20-year-old prediction and could provide a new platform for applied quantum technologies.

science.title: New study reveals children drive cultural change
science.summary: A new study led by our Psychology Department has revealed that children play a much bigger role in shaping human culture than previously thought. The research, published in the journal Behavioral and Brain Sciences, shows that children are not just learning from adults but also creating, sharing and maintaining their own peer cultures.

science.title: Two atmospheric patterns may explain why some heat waves in Europe persist
science.summary: Many parts of Western Europe are currently wilting under a heat wave. These blistering spells can last for a week or more, and although they are common in most summers, it is difficult to predict how long they will last.

science.title: Engineers find a precise way to grow artificial blood vessels
science.summary: Tissue engineers are finding ways to grow living organs and tissues from cells, with the aim of replacing diseased and damaged counterparts in the body. Scientists have successfully grown artificial muscles, livers, kidneys, skin and other tissues. But there's been no reliable way to engineer precisely patterned networks of blood vessels, some of which can be finer than a human hair.

science.title: Modern slavery is a business decision—not an accident
science.summary: Modern slavery persists because the way global supply chains are designed allows it to remain hidden, according to new research led by Professor Glenn Parry from the University of Surrey and Dr. Mike Rogerson at the University of Sussex.

science.title: Statistical test helps judge the value of personalization
science.summary: From precision medicine to personalized job training, customizing interventions for individuals is often assumed to produce better outcomes than a one-size-fits-all approach. But personalization also comes with costs: it can be more expensive, harder to implement reliably and may require more resources to design.

science.title: 35 years of Florida scrub-jay research reveals benefits of long-term pair bonds
science.summary: At Archbold Biological Station, decades of field observations are helping scientists better understand one of nature's oldest relationship strategies: long-term monogamy.

science.title: Widespread misuse of crowd-control weapons by law enforcement at immigration enforcement protests
science.summary: Law enforcement agents misused crowd-control weapons during protests against the Trump administration's immigration enforcement operations in 412 verified incidents across 16 U.S. cities from when immigration enforcement protests escalated in Los Angeles in June 2025 through May 2026, according to Charting the Crackdown, a digital mapping report released today by Physicians for Human Rights (PHR) and the Human Rights Center at the University of California, Berkeley (HRC).

science.title: For time-critical events: NINJA prepares for first on-sky observations
science.summary: This summer, Subaru Telescope will welcome a new near-infrared spectrograph, NINJA, for its first on-sky engineering observations. NINJA is a new spectrograph designed for time-critical observations of rapidly evolving cosmic events, such as the electromagnetic counterparts of gravitational-wave sources.

science.title: Twisted ultrathin magnet retains magnetization after field changes, study finds
science.summary: The properties of ultrathin magnets can be specifically altered by a slight twist between two atomic monolayers. This is the conclusion reached by an international research team led by TU Darmstadt in a study published in Nature Communications. The findings open new prospects for future memory devices.

science.title: As Chagos governance is slated to shift, new research reveals the true scale of fishing
science.summary: Domestic fisheries catches in the Chagos Archipelago are 25 times higher than official statistics show, according to a recent study by Sea Around Us—Indian Ocean and Sea Around Us researchers.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.summary: Salon des Refusés, programul curatorial de recuperare și recontextualizare a filmelor de autor contemporane rămase în afara distribuției comerciale, își extinde oficial activitatea la Timișoara. După succesul edițiilor din București… Articolul Salon des Refusés lansează o ediție specială la Timișoara: Cineclub, proiecții premiate internațional și dialoguri curatoriale la Cinema Studio. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Razie în Vama Veche. A fost prins un bătrân cu 300 de pastile de Antinevralgic la el
mix_items.summary: Razie DIICOT în iadul boșorogilor din Vama Veche. Polițiștii însoțiți de procurori au capturat mai mulți traficanți de stupefiante, inclusiv un bărbat de 51 de ani, care avea la el 300 de pastile de antinevralgic. Bărbatul a declarat că pastilele sunt pentru consum propriu, pentru că stă într-o cameră cu pereți de rigips și în […] The post Razie în Vama Veche. A fost prins un bătrân cu 300 de pastile de Antinevralgic la el appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Primele explicații ale lui Mihai Jurca după ce a renunțat la televiziune după 21 de ani: „Nu iau niciodată decizii pripite, decizii la nervi, decizii la „cald””
mix_items.summary: Cunoscutul prezentator al rubricii meteo de la Antena 1 a explicat pentru PaginadeMedia ce se află în spatele schimbării sale de carieră. Mihai Jurca a prezentat ultima sa rubrică meteo în televiziune, urmând ca în perioada următoare să se alăture agenției de turism lansate de Răzvan Pascu, în rolul de tour leader. „Am avut mereu …

mix_items.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție
mix_items.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: How supermassive black holes feed themselves
mix_items.summary: Astronomers are closer to solving the mystery of how supermassive black holes feed themselves thanks to new images from the James Webb Space Telescope, or JWST. The images provide the clearest view ever seen of gaseous filaments connecting a galaxy's hot atmosphere to the rotating disk of gas that feeds its central supermassive black hole.

mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Bio-metal: Exploring the metallic mystery of an ancient maw
mix_items.summary: When playing the classic game "20 Questions," one may begin with the common opener: "Animal, vegetable, or mineral?"

mix_items.title: The customer isn't always right: Study reveals overlooked source of workplace discrimination
mix_items.summary: New research from Adelaide University has found customers can be an overlooked source of workplace discrimination, leaving gay and bisexual men feeling they must choose between being authentic or protecting themselves from prejudice.

mix_items.title: Six medical societies call for immediate adoption of enhanced radiation protection in fluoroscopy labs
mix_items.summary: Health care professionals performing minimally invasive procedures in fluoroscopy laboratories, often called "cath labs," should no longer have to accept preventable radiation exposure and orthopedic injuries as part of their jobs, according to an expert consensus statement released today that is endorsed by six leading medical societies.

mix_items.title: Preterm birth impacts early educational achievements, study finds
mix_items.summary: More than half (57%) of children born before 32 weeks were not ready for school at age 5, including in areas such as communication and language and physical and emotional development. Those born earlier, at 23–24 weeks, were up to three times more likely to miss expected developmental milestones than those born at 31 weeks.

mix_items.title: Haven or trap? Study finds sinkholes protect endangered tree at evolutionary cost
mix_items.summary: Are giant sinkholes in China's karst mountains havens or traps for the rare plants that inhabit them? A new study finds they are both—offering refuge from heat and drought while gradually eroding the evolutionary potential of an endangered tree.

mix_items.title: Metabolites produced by gut bacteria may protect against fungal infection
mix_items.summary: A metabolic byproduct formed when gut bacteria break down dietary fiber appears to protect against dangerous fungal infections common in immunocompromised patients, a study led by UT Southwestern Medical Center researchers shows. The findings, published in Cell Host & Microbe, could lead to new therapies to shield this vulnerable population from Candida albicans, a leading human fungal pathogen.

mix_items.title: Jude Bellingham's leg veins are standing out as much as his scoring skills: Here's why
mix_items.summary: The ripple of the net after a Jude Bellingham strike is no longer the only ripple attracting attention. During England's World Cup campaign, the midfielder's prominent thigh veins have become an internet talking point.

mix_items.title: Physicists confirm 20-year-old theory that could boost quantum technology
mix_items.summary: Future quantum computing will require correlations between distant modules—a feature known as distributed entanglement. Traditionally, such entanglement has relied on active control and repeated measurements. Now, physicists at the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA) have realized a fully autonomous method for distributed entanglement using a "quantum bath" of correlated light particles. Published in Physical Review X, their work experimentally confirms a 20-year-old prediction and could provide a new platform for applied quantum technologies.

mix_items.title: New study reveals children drive cultural change
mix_items.summary: A new study led by our Psychology Department has revealed that children play a much bigger role in shaping human culture than previously thought. The research, published in the journal Behavioral and Brain Sciences, shows that children are not just learning from adults but also creating, sharing and maintaining their own peer cultures.

mix_items.title: Two atmospheric patterns may explain why some heat waves in Europe persist
mix_items.summary: Many parts of Western Europe are currently wilting under a heat wave. These blistering spells can last for a week or more, and although they are common in most summers, it is difficult to predict how long they will last.

mix_items.title: Engineers find a precise way to grow artificial blood vessels
mix_items.summary: Tissue engineers are finding ways to grow living organs and tissues from cells, with the aim of replacing diseased and damaged counterparts in the body. Scientists have successfully grown artificial muscles, livers, kidneys, skin and other tissues. But there's been no reliable way to engineer precisely patterned networks of blood vessels, some of which can be finer than a human hair.

mix_items.title: Modern slavery is a business decision—not an accident
mix_items.summary: Modern slavery persists because the way global supply chains are designed allows it to remain hidden, according to new research led by Professor Glenn Parry from the University of Surrey and Dr. Mike Rogerson at the University of Sussex.

mix_items.title: Early tech training builds clinical confidence
mix_items.summary: New research from The University of Texas at Arlington suggests that introducing electronic health record training earlier in nurse practitioner programs could help address a significant challenge facing the field—burnout among experienced clinicians who supervise and train future nurse practitioners.

mix_items.title: Statistical test helps judge the value of personalization
mix_items.summary: From precision medicine to personalized job training, customizing interventions for individuals is often assumed to produce better outcomes than a one-size-fits-all approach. But personalization also comes with costs: it can be more expensive, harder to implement reliably and may require more resources to design.

mix_items.title: First diagnostic X-rays in space mark new era for astronaut health
mix_items.summary: A team of crew members aboard a commercial spaceflight acquired the first diagnostic X-rays during an orbital flight. Results of the mission were published today in Radiology.

mix_items.title: 35 years of Florida scrub-jay research reveals benefits of long-term pair bonds
mix_items.summary: At Archbold Biological Station, decades of field observations are helping scientists better understand one of nature's oldest relationship strategies: long-term monogamy.

mix_items.title: The GLP‑1 disclosure dilemma: Should you tell a date you're using Ozempic for weight loss?
mix_items.summary: As a Gen Xer who lived through the "heroin chic" years, when skeletal models were celebrated for jutting bones and sallow skin, the recent popularity of the #Y2KSkinny trend fills me with dread.

mix_items.title: There's one reason why so many cold and flu tablets don't actually work
mix_items.summary: You've got the sniffles, and your nose is running like a faucet. The tissues feel like sandpaper. Many Australians reach for cold and flu tablets, expecting them to "clear the sinuses" and get them through a cold. They often promise fast relief.

mix_items.title: Widespread misuse of crowd-control weapons by law enforcement at immigration enforcement protests
mix_items.summary: Law enforcement agents misused crowd-control weapons during protests against the Trump administration's immigration enforcement operations in 412 verified incidents across 16 U.S. cities from when immigration enforcement protests escalated in Los Angeles in June 2025 through May 2026, according to Charting the Crackdown, a digital mapping report released today by Physicians for Human Rights (PHR) and the Human Rights Center at the University of California, Berkeley (HRC).

mix_items.title: For time-critical events: NINJA prepares for first on-sky observations
mix_items.summary: This summer, Subaru Telescope will welcome a new near-infrared spectrograph, NINJA, for its first on-sky engineering observations. NINJA is a new spectrograph designed for time-critical observations of rapidly evolving cosmic events, such as the electromagnetic counterparts of gravitational-wave sources.

mix_items.title: Twisted ultrathin magnet retains magnetization after field changes, study finds
mix_items.summary: The properties of ultrathin magnets can be specifically altered by a slight twist between two atomic monolayers. This is the conclusion reached by an international research team led by TU Darmstadt in a study published in Nature Communications. The findings open new prospects for future memory devices.

mix_items.title: As Chagos governance is slated to shift, new research reveals the true scale of fishing
mix_items.summary: Domestic fisheries catches in the Chagos Archipelago are 25 times higher than official statistics show, according to a recent study by Sea Around Us—Indian Ocean and Sea Around Us researchers.

mix_items.title: Popular weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy may slow biological aging
mix_items.summary: Researchers found that semaglutide, the active ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy, slowed biological aging markers in adults with HIV, marking the first clinical evidence that the drug may influence human aging. Although the findings are encouraging, scientists say larger studies are needed before concluding that the medication can help people age more slowly.

mix_items.title: What positive change have you seen unfold over many years?
mix_items.summary: Share the stories of change you’ve witnessed that took patience, persistence and time The post What positive change have you seen unfold over many years? appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am setat alarma la 6:00. Ea a venit, eu am dat delegare.

satire_ro.title: Justiţia a decis: biserica ridicată ilegal la Techirghiol trebuie mutată ilegal în altă zonă naturală protejată
